items.q: What does the term jihad mean in Islam?
items.correct: Struggle or striving — the greater jihad is the internal spiritual struggle against sin; the lesser jihad refers to defensive military struggle under strict conditions.
items.random_wrong: Jihad is the Islamic word for prayer.
items.why: The 'holy war' translation is reductive and reflects media usage, not Islamic teaching. The primary meaning is spiritual struggle — military jihad is tightly constrained in classical Islamic law.

items.q: Is the Buddha a god in Buddhism?
items.correct: No — the Buddha (Siddhartha Gautama) is regarded as an enlightened human teacher who discovered the path to liberation. Theravada Buddhism is generally non-theistic; some Mahayana traditions develop more elaborate cosmologies.
items.misconception: Buddhists worship the Buddha as their god.
items.why: Statues and offerings look like worship to students, but classically the Buddha is venerated as teacher, not worshipped as creator god. The path he taught, not his divinity, is what matters in most Buddhist traditions.

items.q: What was the Augustine vs Pelagius dispute about original sin?
items.correct: Augustine argued humans inherit a corrupted nature from Adam (original sin) and cannot achieve salvation without grace; Pelagius argued humans retain free will to choose good without divine intervention and that sin is imitated, not inherited.
items.misconception: Original sin means Christians believe babies are evil.
items.random_wrong: Pelagius and Augustine agreed humans are born innocent.
items.why: Original sin is about inherited nature/condition, not personal guilt of infants. The Pelagian controversy shaped Western Christianity — Augustine won, but the question of how far human nature is corrupted remains theologically contested.

items.q: Which Christian thinker first developed Just War theory?
items.why: Correct answer: Augustine. “Aquinas” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which Just War condition means civilians must not be targeted?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Discrimination. “Just cause” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many Just War conditions (jus ad bellum + jus in bello combined) are typically taught at GCSE?
items.why: The real figure is 8. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
